steps.description: Select configured Luther dogfood issue

parameters.command:
set -euo pipefail

if [ -n "{primary_issue_number}" ] && [ "{primary_issue_number}" != "0" ]; then
  ISSUE_JSON=$(gh issue view {primary_issue_number} --repo {target_repo} --json number,title,state --jq 'select(.state == "OPEN") | {number, title}')
  if [ -n "$ISSUE_JSON" ]; then
    ISSUE_NUMBER=$(echo "$ISSUE_JSON" | jq -r '.number')
    ISSUE_TITLE=$(echo "$ISSUE_JSON" | jq -r '.title')
    if [ "{daemon_managed_claim}" != "true" ]; then
      gh issue edit "$ISSUE_NUMBER" --repo {target_repo} --add-assignee {assignee} --add-label "{luther_label}" > /dev/null
    fi
    jq -n --argjson num "$ISSUE_NUMBER" --arg title "$ISSUE_TITLE" '{number: $num, title: $title}'
    exit 0
  fi
fi


# Query milestones sorted by semver (sort -V)
MILESTONES=$(gh api repos/{target_repo}/milestones --jq '.[].title' | sort -V)

SELECTED_ISSUE=""
FOUND=0
for MILESTONE in $MILESTONES; do
  # List open issues: unassigned, labeled "{ok_label}", not already "{luther_label}"
  ISSUES=$(gh issue list --repo {target_repo} --milestone "$MILESTONE" --state open --assignee "" --label "{ok_label}" --json number,title,labels --limit 50 --jq '[.[] | select(all(.labels[].name; . != "{luther_label}")) | {number, title}] | sort_by(.number)')

  COUNT=$(echo "$ISSUES" | jq 'length')
  if [ "$COUNT" -eq 0 ]; then
    continue
  fi

  for i in $(seq 0 $((COUNT - 1))); do
    NUM=$(echo "$ISSUES" | jq -r ".[$i].number")
    # Skip issues that already have an open PR (REQ-LF-ISSUE-004)
    PR_COUNT=$(gh pr list --repo {target_repo} --search "issue:$NUM" --state open --json number --jq 'length')
    if [ "$PR_COUNT" = "0" ]; then
      SELECTED_ISSUE=$(echo "$ISSUES" | jq -c ".[$i]")
      FOUND=1
      break 2
    fi
  done
done

if [ "$FOUND" = "0" ]; then
  echo "FATAL: No eligible unassigned issues found in any milestone" >&2
  exit 1
fi

ISSUE_NUMBER=$(echo "$SELECTED_ISSUE" | jq -r '.number')
ISSUE_TITLE=$(echo "$SELECTED_ISSUE" | jq -r '.title')

# Claim the issue: assign and label (redirect stdout to avoid polluting JSON output)
gh issue edit "$ISSUE_NUMBER" --repo {target_repo} --add-assignee {assignee} --add-label "{luther_label}" > /dev/null

# Output JSON for context_map extraction (only on success path)
jq -n --argjson num "$ISSUE_NUMBER" --arg title "$ISSUE_TITLE" '{number: $num, title: $title}'
exit 0

steps.description: Ensure repo is checked out, create issue branch, create artifact dir

parameters.command:
set -eu

unset GIT_DIR GIT_WORK_TREE GIT_COMMON_DIR GIT_OBJECT_DIRECTORY GIT_ALTERNATE_OBJECT_DIRECTORIES GIT_INDEX_FILE GIT_CONFIG_COUNT GIT_CONFIG_PARAMETERS GIT_PROXY_COMMAND GIT_SSH GIT_SSH_COMMAND
export GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL=/dev/null GIT_CONFIG_SYSTEM=/dev/null GIT_CONFIG_NOSYSTEM=1
cd "{work_dir}"
EXPECTED_ORIGIN='https://github.com/{target_repo}.git'
if [ -e .luther ] && { [ -L .luther ] || [ ! -d .luther ]; }; then
  echo "Workspace ownership directory must be a real directory" >&2
  exit 1
fi
if [ -f .luther/workspace-owner ] && [ ! -L .luther/workspace-owner ]; then
  if ! printf %s '{run_id}' | cmp -s - .luther/workspace-owner; then
    echo "Workspace ownership marker does not exactly match this run" >&2
    exit 1
  fi
elif [ "{daemon_managed_claim}" = "true" ]; then
  echo "Daemon-managed workspace has no regular ownership marker" >&2
  exit 1
fi
if [ -e .git ] && { [ -L .git ] || [ ! -d .git ]; }; then
  echo "Workspace Git metadata must be a real directory" >&2
  exit 1
fi
if [ -d .git ] && find .git -type l -print -quit | grep -q .; then
  echo "Workspace Git metadata may not contain symlinks" >&2
  exit 1
fi
if [ ! -d .git ] && find . -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .luther -print -quit | grep -q .; then
  echo "Owned workspace contains unexpected pre-repository state" >&2
  exit 1
fi
if [ ! -d .git ]; then
  git -c core.hooksPath=/dev/null init 1>&2
fi
ORIGIN_URL=$(git config --get remote.origin.url || true)
if [ -n "$ORIGIN_URL" ] && [ "$ORIGIN_URL" != "$EXPECTED_ORIGIN" ]; then
  echo "Workspace origin does not match target repository" >&2
  exit 1
fi
rm -f .git/config .git/objects/info/alternates
cat > .git/config <<EOF
[core]
	repositoryformatversion = 0
	filemode = true
	bare = false
	logallrefupdates = true
	hooksPath = /dev/null
[remote "origin"]
	url = $EXPECTED_ORIGIN
	fetch = +refs/heads/*:refs/remotes/origin/*
EOF
if ! git -c core.hooksPath=/dev/null fsck --connectivity-only >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo "Workspace repository failed connectivity verification" >&2
  exit 1
fi
git -c core.hooksPath=/dev/null fetch --prune origin
git -c core.hooksPath=/dev/null for-each-ref --format='delete %(refname)' refs/replace | git -c core.hooksPath=/dev/null update-ref --stdin
EXISTING_PR_JSON=$(gh pr view issue{issue_number} --repo {target_repo} --json number,url,state,isDraft 2>/dev/null || true)
if [ -n "$EXISTING_PR_JSON" ] && [ "$(echo "$EXISTING_PR_JSON" | jq -r '.state')" = "OPEN" ] && [ "$(echo "$EXISTING_PR_JSON" | jq -r '.isDraft')" = "false" ]; then
  EXISTING_PR_NUMBER=$(echo "$EXISTING_PR_JSON" | jq -r '.number')
  EXISTING_PR_URL=$(echo "$EXISTING_PR_JSON" | jq -r '.url')
else
  EXISTING_PR_NUMBER="0"
  EXISTING_PR_URL=""
fi
if git rev-parse --verify -q HEAD >/dev/null; then
  git -c core.hooksPath=/dev/null reset --hard 1>&2
fi
if [ "$EXISTING_PR_NUMBER" != "0" ] && git rev-parse --verify origin/issue{issue_number} >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  git -c core.hooksPath=/dev/null checkout -B issue{issue_number} origin/issue{issue_number} 1>&2
else
  git -c core.hooksPath=/dev/null checkout {base_branch} 1>&2
  git -c core.hooksPath=/dev/null reset --hard origin/{base_branch} 1>&2
  git -c core.hooksPath=/dev/null checkout -B issue{issue_number} 1>&2
fi

mkdir -p {artifact_dir}
rm -f {artifact_dir}/plan.md {artifact_dir}/plan-evaluation.md {artifact_dir}/plan-feedback.md {artifact_dir}/implement-stdout.txt {artifact_dir}/implement-stderr.txt {artifact_dir}/implementation-evaluation.md {artifact_dir}/implementation-evaluation-stdout.txt {artifact_dir}/implementation-evaluation-stderr.txt {artifact_dir}/remediate-output.txt {artifact_dir}/remediate-stdout.txt {artifact_dir}/remediate-stderr.txt {artifact_dir}/verify-report.json {artifact_dir}/pr-description.md
rm -f implementation-evaluation.tmp.md .tmp-implementation-evaluation.md
jq -n --argjson existing_pr_number "$EXISTING_PR_NUMBER" --arg existing_pr_url "$EXISTING_PR_URL" '{existing_pr_number: $existing_pr_number, existing_pr_url: $existing_pr_url}'

steps.description: Route existing PRs directly to post-PR follow-up

parameters.command:
set -euo pipefail
if [ "{setup_workspace.existing_pr_number}" != "0" ]; then
  echo "POST_PR_FOLLOWUP"
else
  echo "PRE_PR_IMPLEMENTATION"
fi

steps.description: Fetch issue body and comments, write to artifact files, extract metadata to context

parameters.command:
set -euo pipefail

# Fetch full issue data as JSON
gh issue view {issue_number} --repo {target_repo} --json title,body,comments,url > {artifact_dir}/issue-raw.json

# Write issue body to file
jq -r '.body // ""' {artifact_dir}/issue-raw.json > {artifact_dir}/issue.md

# Write comments with thread structure preserved (REQ-LF-FETCH-003)
jq -r '.comments[] | "## Comment by \(.author.login) at \(.createdAt)\n\n\(.body)\n\n---\n"' {artifact_dir}/issue-raw.json > {artifact_dir}/comments.md

# Emit short metadata as JSON for context_map
jq '{title: .title, url: .url}' {artifact_dir}/issue-raw.json

steps.description: Prepare a fresh planning attempt while preserving evaluator feedback

parameters.command:
set -euo pipefail
if [ -f {artifact_dir}/plan-evaluation.md ]; then
  cp {artifact_dir}/plan-evaluation.md {artifact_dir}/plan-feedback.md
fi
rm -f {artifact_dir}/plan.md {artifact_dir}/plan-evaluation.md

steps.description: Invoke llxprt to create a plan for the issue

parameters.prompt:
Do not use subagents. Do not ask questions. Read {artifact_dir}/issue.md and {artifact_dir}/comments.md. If {artifact_dir}/plan-feedback.md exists, treat it as required feedback from the prior plan review and address every point in the new plan. Inspect the repository as needed.
Write a concise implementation plan to {artifact_dir}/plan.md. The plan MUST be specific to Luther issue #{issue_number} and the current issue text, must identify the relevant source/config/test files, must describe the architectural problem from the issue, and must list the intended tests. Do not write placeholder plans.
If a requested change or existing code hits file-size, complexity, or structural guardrails, the plan must propose semantic decomposition by responsibility: cohesive mod submodules with narrow visibility (private or pub(crate)/pub(super)) and focused tests. Do not propose textual source stitching, include!()-based Rust module assembly, numbered fragments (part_N, core_N), tail files, broad support/misc/utils buckets, lint suppression, or threshold changes. Explain the domain boundaries and visibility model for each new module before implementation.
You are working on the Luther workflow repository itself in a separate target checkout. Do not implement the fix in this step. Exit after writing {artifact_dir}/plan.md.

steps.description: Invoke llxprt to evaluate the plan

parameters.prompt: Do not use subagents. Do not ask questions. Read the issue in {artifact_dir}/issue.md and the plan in {artifact_dir}/plan.md. Evaluate whether the plan adequately addresses the issue and is narrowly scoped. Reject placeholder, generic, underspecified, or shell-unsafe plans. Reject plans that move code into numbered chunks (part_N, core_N), tail files, or generic support/misc/utils buckets; that use include!() for Rust source module assembly; that preserve a monolithic module behind physical file splitting; that loosen guardrails or add suppression directives; or that create public helper APIs only to dodge module boundaries. Require the plan to explain why each proposed module is cohesive and to use the narrowest viable visibility. Plans that move target-specific shell behavior into profile-provided raw shell snippets inside workflow command strings are not safe; require structured argv/path configuration or executor support that keeps workflow command lines statically allowlisted. If the plan is ready, respond with exactly PLAN_APPROVED on its own line. If it needs revision, respond with exactly PLAN_NEEDS_REVISION on its own line, followed by your feedback. Write any detailed feedback to {artifact_dir}/plan-evaluation.md, then exit.

steps.description: Deterministically verify plan evaluator approval before implementation

parameters.command:
set -euo pipefail
if [ -f {artifact_dir}/plan-evaluation.md ] && grep -q '^PLAN_NEEDS_REVISION$' {artifact_dir}/plan-evaluation.md; then
  echo 'PLAN_REJECTED'
  exit 0
fi
if [ ! -s {artifact_dir}/plan.md ]; then
  echo 'PLAN_REJECTED'
  exit 0
fi
PLAN_BYTES=$(tr -d '[:space:]' < {artifact_dir}/plan.md | wc -c | tr -d ' ')
if [ "$PLAN_BYTES" -lt 200 ]; then
  echo 'PLAN_REJECTED'
  exit 0
fi
echo 'PLAN_GATE_APPROVED'

steps.description: Preflight workflow-file authorization from plan scope
Freeze scope-control task charter before mutable implementation
Invoke llxprt to implement the plan

parameters.prompt:
Do not use subagents. Do not ask questions. Read the current issue in {artifact_dir}/issue.md and the approved plan in {artifact_dir}/plan.md, then implement that plan.
CRITICAL WORKING METHOD — write first, read minimally:
- The approved plan already lists the exact files and line references you need. Trust the plan. Do NOT try to fully understand the entire codebase before writing.
- Your FIRST tool action after reading the plan MUST be to create or modify a file (write the new file skeleton, or make the first concrete edit). Do not spend the early part of this step reading: a long sequence of read-only file inspections with no writes is the primary failure mode of this step and is forbidden.
- Read at most a handful of targeted file ranges, and only to confirm an exact API signature or the specific lines you are about to edit. If you find yourself reading a fourth or fifth file before your first write, stop reading and start writing instead.
- Build incrementally: create the file(s) with an initial version early so a non-empty git diff exists, then iterate to fill in details. It is far better to have a scoped, compiling first draft than to keep reading.
- You have a generous time budget, but exploration does not count as progress. Only written edits do.
Make the smallest source/config/test changes needed to fix Luther issue #{issue_number}. You MUST leave a non-empty git diff in {work_dir}; if no files are changed, this step is not complete. Do not modify package lockfiles, generated notice files, .luther files, or unrelated files.
The required source and test scope is whatever the approved plan identifies for this issue. If the plan and issue disagree, follow the issue text and update only files directly relevant to the current issue.
Add or update focused tests and fixtures when the issue or approved plan requires them. Do not apply requirements from a different issue; for issue #{issue_number}, completion means the changed files directly satisfy the current issue text and approved plan, whether those are source, config, fixture, or test files.
REPOSITORY CONVENTIONS THE VERIFICATION GATE ENFORCES (honor these as you write, because the gate runs clippy with -D warnings and a strict test suite):
- Clippy denies too_many_lines: every function, INCLUDING test functions, must stay at or under 100 lines. If a new or edited fn would exceed 100 lines, split it into helper functions or smaller #[test] cases before finishing. Also honor the other strict lints (cognitive_complexity, too_many_arguments, type_complexity).
- Fixture parity and fixture validity: the directories workflow/tests/fixtures/workflows/valid and workflow/tests/fixtures/workflow-configs/valid require a matching .json fixture for every .toml fixture (and vice versa); a parity test fails otherwise. Existing resolvable workflow fixtures outside valid/ (for example workflow/tests/fixtures/workflows/issue-fix-v1.toml) must also remain valid when you add validation rules. Whenever you add a validation rule, update every production workflow, every valid fixture, and every existing resolvable test fixture that the rule now constrains. Whenever you add a .toml fixture under a valid/ directory, add the equivalent .json fixture in the same step. Put intentionally invalid fixtures under an invalid/ directory instead of valid/. If you add a new workflow step type, register a fixture executor wherever integration tests construct a test-only ExecutorRegistry, and update explicit step-count assertions/fixtures that load the affected production workflow.
Do not run full project verification commands such as cargo test, cargo build, or cargo clippy. The workflow runs configured checks after this step. You may run narrowly targeted tests/checks related to the files you changed.
If a shell command you wrote fails because of quoting, heredoc, or syntax problems, correct the command and continue. Do not treat agent-authored shell syntax mistakes as implementation or workflow system errors while simple corrected shell commands still work.
When editing workflow shell commands, keep command lines statically allowlisted and shell-safe. Do not insert raw profile-provided shell snippets or arbitrary command templates into workflow command strings; use structured argv/path variables or source-code executor support instead.
Treat file-size, complexity, clippy, or structural-guardrail failures as design feedback, not as obstacles to bypass. Fix the underlying structure: split code into semantic mod submodules with cohesive responsibilities (named after the responsibility, not a line offset) and the narrowest viable visibility (private, pub(crate), or pub(super)), with focused tests co-located with the code. Do not split files by line count. Do not use include!() to reassemble Rust source modules. Do not create part_N, core_N, tail, misc, utils, or support buckets unless the name describes a real domain concept. Do not add lint-disable/suppression attributes or change complexity/size thresholds merely to make a module fit. If a submodule is still oversized, split it further along a real responsibility seam.

If {artifact_dir}/implementation-evaluation.md exists, treat it as a required remediation checklist and address every unresolved item before finishing this step. Do not stop after partially addressing the feedback.
Before responding, inspect git diff --stat and git diff. If the diff does not match the current issue and approved plan, continue working or remove the unrelated changes before finishing. Remove any accidental temporary files in {work_dir} before finishing.
Finish as soon as source/test edits are present and you have inspected the diff. Do not keep iterating on targeted tests after making a scoped diff; the workflow will run deterministic verification and remediation steps. Finish with exactly IMPLEMENTATION_COMPLETE on its own line, followed by a concise truthful summary of the changes and verification you performed, then exit.

steps.description: Subjective review: does the implementation actually solve the issue per plan? (runs AFTER lint/typecheck/test/build pass)

parameters.prompt:
Do not use subagents. Do not ask questions. All configured deterministic checks have already passed.
This workflow step is not a broad audit or design review. It is a narrow issue-acceptance gate for the current branch diff.
Your job is ONLY subjective correctness: read the issue in {artifact_dir}/issue.md and the plan in {artifact_dir}/plan.md, then inspect the uncommitted working-tree diff with git status --porcelain --untracked-files=all, git diff --stat HEAD, and git diff HEAD. This step runs before push_changes commits the branch, so origin/{base_branch}...HEAD may be empty even when the implementation is present.
Judge only: does this working-tree implementation materially solve the current issue text and approved plan? Verify the diff is scoped to issue #{issue_number}. Do not require the exact approach from the plan if the behavior is correct, tested, and narrowly scoped. Reject implementations that use include!() for Rust source module assembly, numbered split files (part_N, core_N), tail files, generic support/misc/utils buckets introduced only to satisfy file-size limits, guardrail loosening, or suppression directives; require cohesive mod submodules with narrow visibility instead.

Do NOT re-flag style, formatting, or test issues - those are handled elsewhere.
If the implementation correctly addresses the issue and plan, respond with exactly IMPL_APPROVED on its own line.
If the implementation does not actually solve the issue or materially diverges from the plan, respond with exactly IMPL_NEEDS_WORK on its own line, followed by concise feedback, and also write detailed actionable feedback to {artifact_dir}/implementation-evaluation.md.
If you cannot perform this narrow acceptance gate because of instruction conflict or tool/runtime limitations, respond with exactly IMPL_EVALUATION_BLOCKED on its own line followed by the blocker; do not write implementation-evaluation.md in that case.

steps.description: Run lint, typecheck, test, format, build checks

diff_or_existing_pr.failure_message: No Luther workflow/config/test/doc diff found and no reusable open PR exists

steps.description: Measure patch growth and enforce the scope barrier after implementation
Enforce the scope barrier on the final patch before push
Invoke llxprt to remediate verification or evaluator feedback

parameters.prompt:
Do not use subagents. Do not ask questions. Read the current issue in {artifact_dir}/issue.md, the approved plan in {artifact_dir}/plan.md, the verification report in {artifact_dir}/verify-report.json if present, and {artifact_dir}/implementation-evaluation.md if present.
These artifact paths are intentionally outside the target repository. If file-reading tools cannot read an absolute artifact path, inspect it with shell commands such as cat, sed, or python3 instead; do not report an artifact-path access constraint while shell access works. Missing optional artifacts such as implementation-evaluation.md are not blockers.

Start by inspecting git status --porcelain --untracked-files=all, git diff --stat HEAD, git diff HEAD, and any failing check messages. This step runs before push_changes commits the branch, so origin/{base_branch}...HEAD may be empty even when the implementation is present. Use commands that avoid option-like printf formats, such as echo separators or printf '%s
' '--- heading ---'. Focus only on errors or evaluator feedback caused by the current branch diff and issue #{issue_number}; do not try to fix pre-existing repository-wide warnings unrelated to the current diff.
To remediate efficiently, reproduce the failing check yourself (for a Rust target run cargo clippy --manifest-path workflow/Cargo.toml --all-targets and cargo test --manifest-path workflow/Cargo.toml) so you see the COMPLETE list of errors at once instead of guessing from a truncated report. When the same compiler error repeats across many call sites (for example a new struct field reported as missing at dozens of literals, or a renamed symbol), fix every occurrence in a single bulk pass using a scripted edit such as grep -rl plus sed, then recompile to confirm the whole class is gone; do not fix such repeated errors one site at a time. Prefer an implementation shape that minimizes mechanical fan-out: if adding a field to a widely constructed struct, derive or use Default and `..Default::default()`, or update all construction sites in one pass within this same step. For clippy too_many_lines failures, split the offending function or test into helper functions or smaller tests immediately; do not try to work around the lint with attributes. If tests report no executor registered for a new step type, register a fixture executor in the test-only registry and update explicit step-count expectations rather than removing the production workflow step. For fixture parity failures under workflow/tests/fixtures/**/valid, add the missing TOML or JSON sibling (or move intentionally invalid fixtures to invalid/) in the same remediation pass. If a new validation rule makes an existing resolvable fixture invalid, update every affected production workflow, valid fixture, and existing resolvable test fixture (including workflow/tests/fixtures/workflows/issue-fix-v1.toml and its JSON siblings when applicable) in one pass. You must drive the failing checks fully to green before responding REMEDIATION_COMPLETE; a partial reduction in the error count is not success.
If a shell command you wrote fails because of quoting, heredoc, or syntax problems, correct the command and continue. Do not emit REMEDIATION_SYSTEM_ERROR for agent-authored shell syntax mistakes while simple corrected shell commands still work.
When editing workflow shell commands, keep command lines statically allowlisted and shell-safe. Do not insert raw profile-provided shell snippets or arbitrary command templates into workflow command strings; use structured argv/path variables or source-code executor support instead.
Remediate review feedback and guardrail failures architecturally. If the simplest patch would satisfy a checker while preserving a bad abstraction (for example a file-size or structural failure), stop and produce a short decomposition plan, then implement cohesive mod submodules named after their responsibility with narrow visibility and co-located focused tests. Do not use include!() to reassemble Rust modules, do not split files by line count, and do not introduce part_N/core_N/tail/misc/utils/support buckets. Passing CI is necessary but not sufficient; the code must remain navigable, cohesive, and honestly modular.

Fix the current branch so it both materially solves the issue and lets the configured checks pass. For issue #{issue_number}, do not stop until production workflow/config, matching valid fixtures, and workflow/tests/e2e_workflow_integration.rs are updated consistently. Do not modify package lockfiles, generated notice files, .luther files, or unrelated files unless required by the issue. If implementation-evaluation.md reports unrelated committed branch artifacts, remove those artifacts from the branch even if plain git diff is otherwise empty.
Before responding, inspect git status --porcelain --untracked-files=all, git diff --stat HEAD, and git diff HEAD again. You are not complete if the working-tree diff is empty, if unrelated files remain in the working-tree diff, if fixtures/tests are missing for issue #{issue_number}, or if implementation-evaluation.md still lists unresolved required actions.
If there is no current working-tree diff, first make the minimal issue-specific source/test edit from the plan instead of only inspecting files; do not emit REMEDIATION_SYSTEM_ERROR merely because the previous implementation step stopped early without changes.

If the current branch already contains the issue-specific diff and there are no verification/evaluation failure artifacts to address, inspect git diff and respond with exactly REMEDIATION_COMPLETE on its own line as the final output.

If you successfully fix all current verification/evaluation issues, respond with exactly REMEDIATION_COMPLETE on its own line as the final output.
If you encounter a persistent system/workflow/tooling problem outside your own command syntax (missing profile/tool, broken shell after a simple corrected command, inaccessible required artifacts, etc.), respond with exactly REMEDIATION_SYSTEM_ERROR on its own line, followed by a brief reason.
Do not output shell snippets with unmatched quotes.

steps.description: Preflight workflow-file authorization for pending push diff
Stage, commit, and push changes

parameters.command:
set -euo pipefail
git add -A -- ':!.llxprt' ':!.llxprt/**' ':!.luther' ':!.luther/**'
if git diff --cached --quiet --exit-code; then
  if [ "{setup_workspace.existing_pr_number}" != "0" ]; then
    echo "No new repository changes; existing PR #{setup_workspace.existing_pr_number} already carries issue branch changes."
    exit 0
  fi
  echo "No repository changes to commit" >&2
  exit 1
fi
git commit -m "Fix #{issue_number}: {issue_title}"
git push --force-with-lease -u origin issue{issue_number}

steps.description: Invoke llxprt to write a PR description

parameters.prompt: Do not use subagents. Do not ask questions. Read the issue in {artifact_dir}/issue.md and the plan in {artifact_dir}/plan.md. Review the code changes (use git diff origin/{base_branch}). Write a PR description to {artifact_dir}/pr-description.md. The description MUST include 'Fixes #{issue_number}' to auto-close the issue, then exit.

steps.description: Create a GitHub pull request

parameters.command:
set -euo pipefail
EXISTING_PR_JSON=$(gh pr view issue{issue_number} --repo {target_repo} --json number,url,title,state,isDraft 2>/dev/null || true)
if [ -n "$EXISTING_PR_JSON" ] && [ "$(echo "$EXISTING_PR_JSON" | jq -r '.state')" = "OPEN" ] && [ "$(echo "$EXISTING_PR_JSON" | jq -r '.isDraft')" = "false" ]; then
  PR_JSON=$(echo "$EXISTING_PR_JSON" | jq '{number,url,title,state}')
else
  PR_URL=$(gh pr create --repo {target_repo} --title "Fix #{issue_number}: {issue_title}" --body-file {artifact_dir}/pr-description.md --base {base_branch} --head issue{issue_number})
  PR_JSON=$(gh pr view "$PR_URL" --repo {target_repo} --json number,url,title,state)
fi
printf '%s
' "$PR_JSON"

steps.description: Capture pull request identity and current head metadata
Enforce artifact-backed PR follow-up remediation iteration cap
Watch GitHub PR checks and classify current-head status
Collect concrete CI failures or route untrusted check states to terminal failure
Collect ready CodeRabbit feedback for deterministic evaluation
Evaluate CodeRabbit feedback with bounded validated LLM decisions
Build deterministic remediation plan from CI failures and evaluated feedback
Invoke dedicated PR follow-up remediation wrapper
Validate structured remediation result and artifact-backed retry caps
Run deterministic post-PR local verification after remediation
Stage, commit, and push deterministic remediation changes

parameters.commit_message: Apply PR follow-up remediation for issue #{issue_number}

steps.description: Comment on and resolve CodeRabbit feedback according to deterministic marker plan
Write terminal PR follow-up failure artifact and fail the run
Comment on issue, remove label, unassign on failure

parameters.command:
set -euo pipefail
ISSUE_NUM="{issue_number}"
case "$ISSUE_NUM" in
  "{"*|"")
    if [ -n "{primary_issue_number}" ] && [ "{primary_issue_number}" != "0" ]; then
      ISSUE_NUM="{primary_issue_number}"
    else
      echo "No issue was selected; skipping cleanup."
      exit 0
    fi
    ;;
esac
ABANDON_BODY_FILE="$(mktemp "${TMPDIR:-/tmp}/luther-abandon.XXXXXX")"
COMMENTS_FILE="$(mktemp "${TMPDIR:-/tmp}/luther-comments.XXXXXX")"
trap 'rm -f "$ABANDON_BODY_FILE" "$COMMENTS_FILE"' EXIT
ABANDON_MARKER="<!-- luther-failure-cleanup:{run_id}:{failed_work_step} -->"
printf '%s
' "$ABANDON_MARKER" "Luther dogfood abandoning this issue: workflow failed at step {failed_work_step} with outcome {failed_work_outcome}. Detailed diagnostics remain in the run artifacts." > "$ABANDON_BODY_FILE"
gh issue view "$ISSUE_NUM" --repo {target_repo} --json comments --jq '.comments[].body' > "$COMMENTS_FILE"
if ! grep -Fq "$ABANDON_MARKER" "$COMMENTS_FILE"; then
  gh issue comment "$ISSUE_NUM" --repo {target_repo} --body-file "$ABANDON_BODY_FILE"
fi
if [ "{daemon_managed_claim}" != "true" ] || [ "{claim_label_added}" = "true" ]; then
  gh issue edit "$ISSUE_NUM" --repo {target_repo} --remove-label "{luther_label}"
fi
if [ "{daemon_managed_claim}" != "true" ] || [ "{claim_assignment_added}" = "true" ]; then
  gh issue edit "$ISSUE_NUM" --repo {target_repo} --remove-assignee {assignee}
fi

steps.description: Log successful PR creation

parameters.command: echo "Luther dogfood PR created successfully for issue #{issue_number}: {pr_url}"
